stories.title: Amaterasu and the Cave of the Sun
stories.hook: The sun disappeared, and the world was left in darkness.
stories.texture: With the sun gone, no command could simply make her return. The other gods had to make the world feel alive again.
facts: Amaterasu hid in a cave after a conflict with Susanoo.
The world was plunged into darkness.
The other kami held a celebration to draw her out.
When Amaterasu returned, light returned with her.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.variantNote: Mark regional and later folkloric variants.

stories.title: Anubis and the Weighing of the Heart
stories.hook: After death, one question remained: was your heart lighter than a feather?
stories.texture: Nothing could be argued away there. A person’s own heart was the thing placed on the scale.
facts: Anubis guided the judgment of the dead.
A heart was weighed against the feather of Maat.
The feather represented truth, balance, and justice.
The result decided the dead person’s fate.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.variantNote: Accounts change across long historical periods.

stories.title: Arachne and Athena
stories.hook: Arachne was so certain of her talent that she challenged a goddess to prove it.
stories.texture: Her skill was undeniable, which made the challenge feel less like a boast and more like a collision waiting to happen.
facts: Arachne was famous for her weaving.
She challenged Athena to a contest.
Her tapestry offended the goddess.
In the traditional tale, she became a spider.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Atrahasis and the Flood
stories.hook: Atrahasis heard a warning no one else was meant to hear: a flood was coming, and the gods had already decided the outcome.
stories.texture: The terror is not only the water. It is the knowledge that survival depends on acting before anyone believes you.
facts: The gods decided to send a flood against humanity in the Atrahasis Epic.
Enki warned Atrahasis through a wall or reed hut in the surviving tradition.
Atrahasis built a boat and preserved living creatures.
After the flood, the gods established limits on human population.

sources.title: Atrahasis Epic
sources.variantNote: Do not merge it uncritically with later flood traditions.

stories.title: The Death of Baldr
stories.hook: The gods tried to make Baldr untouchable, but one small thing slipped through their protection.
stories.texture: The tragedy cuts deeper because everyone believed they had already removed every danger from Baldr’s path.
facts: Baldr was loved by the gods and feared dreams of death.
Frigg asked things in the world not to harm him.
Mistletoe was overlooked in many versions.
A mistletoe weapon caused Baldr’s death through Loki’s plot.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Chang’e and the Moon
stories.hook: One drink of immortality changed Chang’e’s life and placed her beyond reach.
stories.texture: The gift of immortality is not presented as simple freedom. It also creates distance that cannot be undone.
facts: Hou Yi received an elixir connected to immortality.
Chang’e drank it in a well-known version of the tale.
She rose to the moon.
She is associated with the moon in later tradition.

sources.title: Classic of Mountains and Seas
sources.variantNote: Separate early textual traditions from later folk retellings.

stories.title: The Children of Lir
stories.hook: Four children were turned into swans and left to watch centuries pass from the water.
stories.texture: Their punishment was not one dramatic moment. It was the slow ache of watching generations disappear around them.
facts: The children of Lir were transformed into swans by their stepmother in Irish tradition.
They were condemned to wander for centuries.
They kept their human voices and memories.
Their story ends when the enchantment is broken, too late for their old lives.

sources.title: Táin Bó Cúailnge
sources.variantNote: Use an identified Irish or Welsh tradition, not the catch-all “Celtic”.

stories.title: The Name of Cú Chulainn
stories.hook: Before he became a legend, Sétanta made one promise that changed his name forever.
stories.texture: For a child, it was an impossible debt: a dead guard dog had left a household unprotected.
facts: As a child, he was called Sétanta.
He accidentally killed Culann’s guard dog.
He promised to take the animal’s place until another could be found.
That is why he became Cú Chulainn, the Hound of Culann.

sources.title: Táin Bó Cúailnge
sources.variantNote: Use an identified Irish or Welsh tradition, not the catch-all “Celtic”.

stories.title: Deirdre of the Sorrows
stories.hook: Before Deirdre could choose her own life, a prophecy had already turned her future into a political prize.
stories.texture: Her escape is not a simple romance. Every mile away from Ulster carries the shadow of the court she left behind.
facts: A prophecy said Deirdre would bring sorrow to Ulster.
Conchobar planned to marry her when she grew older.
Deirdre fled with Naoise and his brothers.
Their return to Ulster ended in betrayal and tragedy.

sources.title: Longes mac nUislenn
sources.variantNote: Irish tale; present it as a tragic cycle, not generic Celtic lore.

stories.title: Fionn and the Salmon of Knowledge
stories.hook: Fionn was only meant to cook a salmon, not taste the knowledge hidden inside it.
stories.texture: The knowledge arrives by accident, which is why the moment feels more like fate than a prize he earned.
facts: A salmon was said to contain all knowledge.
The poet Finn Eces told the young Fionn to cook it.
Fionn burned his thumb and put it in his mouth.
By tasting the salmon, he gained its wisdom.

sources.title: Táin Bó Cúailnge
sources.variantNote: Use an identified Irish or Welsh tradition, not the catch-all “Celtic”.

stories.title: The Origin of Ganesha
stories.hook: Ganesha was created to guard a door, but the person approaching it did not know who he was.
stories.texture: The tragedy begins because neither side understands who is standing at the door or why he refuses to move.
facts: Ganesha is recognized by his elephant head.
In one version, Parvati created him to guard her entrance.
Shiva did not recognize him and tragedy followed.
He was restored to life with a new head and granted honor.

sources.title: Ramayana
sources.variantNote: Identify the epic or Purana because versions vary widely.

stories.title: Gilgamesh and the Search for Immortality
stories.hook: After losing Enkidu, Gilgamesh stopped looking for glory and started looking for a way to escape death.
stories.texture: His search becomes painful because every answer comes after he has already learned how fragile a life can be.
facts: Gilgamesh sought to defeat death after losing Enkidu.
He traveled to find Utnapishtim.
He discovered a plant that could restore youth.
A serpent took the plant before he could use it.

sources.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
sources.variantNote: Do not merge Sumerian, Akkadian, and Babylonian variants without attribution.

stories.title: Hanuman and the Mountain of Herbs
stories.hook: With Lakshmana’s life at stake, Hanuman had no time to choose the right healing plant.
stories.texture: There is no time for perfect certainty when someone’s life is slipping away. Action has to come first.
facts: Hanuman aided Rama in the Ramayana.
He searched for a healing plant to save Lakshmana.
Unable to identify it, he carried the entire mountain.
The episode highlights his loyalty, strength, and determination.

sources.title: Ramayana
sources.variantNote: Identify the epic or Purana because versions vary widely.

stories.title: The Eye of Ra
stories.hook: When Ra sent his eye to punish humanity, the punishment nearly became unstoppable.
stories.texture: Once violence was unleashed, even Ra had to find a way to stop the force he had sent out.
facts: Ra sent his eye to punish people who rebelled.
The eye appeared as a fierce goddess in different versions.
The destruction became so severe that Ra regretted it.
Beer dyed red was used to calm the raging goddess.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.variantNote: Accounts change across long historical periods.

stories.title: Horus and Seth
stories.hook: After Osiris fell, Egypt’s throne became the prize in a bitter struggle.
stories.texture: This was more than a family fight; the struggle asked who could bring order back after murder and chaos.
facts: Horus and Seth contended after the death of Osiris.
Their conflict concerned the right to rule Egypt.
The gods judged their rivalry in different versions.
Horus eventually became the model of rightful kingship.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.variantNote: Accounts change across long historical periods.

stories.title: Hou Yi and the Ten Suns
stories.hook: Ten suns rose together, and the earth began to burn beneath them.
stories.texture: The earth does not need every sun. It needs one, and someone brave enough to make that terrible choice.
facts: Ten suns once rose together and scorched the earth.
The archer Hou Yi was sent to stop the disaster.
He shot down nine suns.
He left one sun so the world would still have light.

sources.title: Classic of Mountains and Seas
sources.variantNote: Separate early textual traditions from later folk retellings.

stories.title: Huitzilopochtli and Coyolxauhqui
stories.hook: Before his first breath, Huitzilopochtli was already surrounded by enemies.
stories.texture: The threat arrives before the hero can even enter the world, turning birth itself into a battlefield.
facts: Coatlicue became pregnant after a ball of feathers touched her.
Her daughter Coyolxauhqui led siblings against her.
Huitzilopochtli emerged fully armed at his birth.
He defeated the attackers in a foundational Mexica myth.

sources.title: Florentine Codex
sources.variantNote: Name the tradition as Nahua or Mexica when the source warrants it.

sources.title: Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas

stories.title: Daedalus and Icarus
stories.hook: Icarus was free at last, with the whole sky above him and one warning below it.
stories.texture: The escape was real, but the sky gave Icarus nowhere to hide from the result of one reckless choice.
facts: Daedalus made wings from feathers and wax to escape Crete.
He warned Icarus not to fly too close to the sun.
Icarus rose higher despite the warning.
The wax melted and he fell into the sea.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Iðunn and the Apples of Youth
stories.hook: The gods only noticed Iðunn was gone when they began to grow old.
stories.texture: The gods had treated youth as permanent, until Iðunn’s absence made time visible on every face.
facts: Iðunn guarded apples that kept the gods young.
The giant Thjazi forced Loki to bring her outside Asgard.
Without the apples, the gods began to age.
Loki helped bring Iðunn back to Asgard.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Inanna’s Descent to the Underworld
stories.hook: Inanna walked into the underworld knowing every gate would take something from her.
stories.texture: At every gate, status and protection were stripped away. By the end, even a goddess stood without power.
facts: Inanna descended into the realm of her sister Ereshkigal.
At each gate she gave up part of her attributes.
She died and was later rescued.
The myth explores power, loss, and transformation.

sources.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
sources.variantNote: Do not merge Sumerian, Akkadian, and Babylonian variants without attribution.

stories.title: Ishtar and the Bull of Heaven
stories.hook: Gilgamesh rejected a goddess, and the answer came with the force of heaven behind it.
stories.texture: A refusal that might have been personal suddenly became a danger for an entire city.
facts: Ishtar asked Gilgamesh to become her lover.
Gilgamesh refused and recalled the suffering of her past partners.
Ishtar sent the Bull of Heaven against Uruk.
Gilgamesh and Enkidu defeated the bull.

sources.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
sources.variantNote: Do not merge Sumerian, Akkadian, and Babylonian variants without attribution.

stories.title: Izanagi’s Journey to Yomi
stories.hook: Izanagi entered the land of the dead for the person he could not bear to lose.
stories.texture: The journey turns on a familiar fear: loving someone enough to follow them, then fearing what you find.
facts: Izanagi went to Yomi to bring back Izanami.
He was told not to look at her.
He broke the promise and saw her transformed by death.
He fled and sealed the entrance to the underworld.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.variantNote: Mark regional and later folkloric variants.

stories.title: Jason and the Golden Fleece
stories.hook: Jason was told he could reclaim a kingdom, but first he had to steal a prize guarded at the edge of the world.
stories.texture: The voyage gathers heroes, but the real danger begins when ambition turns into a test no strength alone can solve.
facts: Jason sailed with the Argonauts to seek the Golden Fleece.
King Aeëtes set tasks that included yoking fire-breathing bulls.
Medea helped Jason in versions where she falls in love with him.
Jason escaped with the Fleece after overcoming the tasks.

sources.title: Apollonius of Rhodes, Argonautica
sources.variantNote: Later retellings differ in Medea’s role.

stories.title: Jingwei Fills the Sea
stories.hook: A girl drowned in the sea, then returned as a bird with one impossible plan: fill the ocean itself.
stories.texture: Jingwei cannot win by force. What makes the image endure is the tiny weight of each twig carried against something vast.
facts: In the tale, a girl drowned in the Eastern Sea.
She became the bird Jingwei.
Jingwei carried twigs and stones toward the sea.
Her endless labor is an act of defiance against the sea that drowned her.

sources.title: Classic of Mountains and Seas
sources.variantNote: Early mythic text; avoid later embellishments.

stories.title: Krishna and Kaliya
stories.hook: A river had become so poisoned that no one could safely enter it, until Krishna stepped into the water.
stories.texture: The fight is remembered for its impossible image: not a warrior standing over a serpent, but dancing on the heads of one.
facts: The serpent Kaliya poisoned the waters of the Yamuna in the story.
Krishna entered the river to confront him.
Krishna danced upon Kaliya’s hoods.
Kaliya was spared after he submitted and left the river.

sources.title: Bhagavata Purana
sources.variantNote: Present this as a Purāṇic telling.

stories.title: How Sleipnir Was Born
stories.hook: A bargain to protect Asgard was about to cost the gods far more than they expected.
stories.texture: What began as a clever construction deal became a race against a deadline the gods could not afford to lose.
facts: A builder promised to fortify Asgard in exchange for great rewards.
His horse Svaðilfari made the work progress too quickly.
Loki intervened so the bargain would fail.
That story led to Sleipnir, Odin’s eight-legged horse.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Marduk and Tiamat
stories.hook: When chaos took the shape of Tiamat, the gods needed someone willing to face it.
stories.texture: The other gods offered Marduk power because they were too frightened to face the chaos themselves.
facts: Tiamat became a great force of chaos in the Enuma Elish.
The gods sought a champion to face her.
Marduk defeated Tiamat after receiving authority.
He used her divided body to order the cosmos.

sources.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
sources.variantNote: Do not merge Sumerian, Akkadian, and Babylonian variants without attribution.

stories.title: The Golden Touch of Midas
stories.hook: King Midas thought he had asked for the perfect gift, until he tried to eat.
stories.texture: Gold had always looked like security from a distance. In his hands, it became the opposite.
facts: Midas was granted a wish by Dionysus.
He asked that everything he touched turn to gold.
Food and drink also became gold in his hands.
He begged to be freed from the gift.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Nanahuatzin Becomes the Sun
stories.hook: When the gods needed a new sun, the humble one moved before the powerful one could.
stories.texture: The contrast is the point: the one with less status finds the courage that the celebrated candidate cannot.
facts: The gods needed someone to become the new sun.
The wealthy Tecuciztecatl hesitated before the sacred fire.
The humble Nanahuatzin leapt in first.
He became the sun, while Tecuciztecatl became the moon in the story.

sources.title: Florentine Codex
sources.variantNote: Name the tradition as Nahua or Mexica when the source warrants it.

sources.title: Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas

stories.title: Nüwa Repairs the Sky
stories.hook: When the sky itself broke, Nüwa had to repair the world before it collapsed completely.
stories.texture: The scale of the disaster is cosmic, but the response is practical: repair what has broken before everything falls.
facts: A catastrophe damaged the pillars supporting the sky.
Nüwa melted five-colored stones to repair the heavens.
She used the legs of a giant turtle as new supports.
The tale presents her as a restorer of cosmic order.

sources.title: Classic of Mountains and Seas
sources.variantNote: Separate early textual traditions from later folk retellings.

stories.title: Odin’s Sacrifice for Wisdom
stories.hook: Odin wanted wisdom badly enough to pay for it with part of himself.
stories.texture: Knowledge in this telling is never free. The question is always what someone is willing to lose for it.
facts: Odin wanted to drink from Mímir’s well.
He gave one of his eyes as payment.
The myth connects him with the pursuit of knowledge.
Odin is also linked to runes and poetry.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Ōkuninushi and the Hare of Inaba
stories.hook: A wounded hare lay on the shore after one clever trick went terribly wrong.
stories.texture: Everyone who passed by had an answer, but only one response understood that healing starts with listening instead of showing off.
facts: A hare tricked sharks to cross the sea and was left injured.
Ōkuninushi’s brothers gave the hare painful advice.
Ōkuninushi told the hare to wash in fresh water and roll in pollen.
The healed hare predicted that Ōkuninushi would win a princess.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.variantNote: Keep the story’s healing sequence intact.

stories.title: Orpheus and Eurydice
stories.hook: Orpheus was given an impossible bargain: lead Eurydice out of death, but never look back.
stories.texture: He had already crossed a boundary no living musician was supposed to cross, and the way back demanded trust.
facts: Orpheus descended to the underworld for Eurydice.
His music moved Hades and Persephone.
He was allowed to lead her out without looking back.
He looked back too soon and lost her again.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Osiris, Isis, and the Return to Life
stories.hook: Seth killed Osiris, but Isis refused to let the story end there.
stories.texture: Isis turns grief into action, searching through loss instead of accepting the violence done to Osiris.
facts: Osiris was killed by his brother Seth in a central tradition.
Isis gathered his body with the help of magic.
Horus was born to confront Seth.
Osiris became ruler of the world of the dead.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.variantNote: Accounts change across long historical periods.

stories.title: Pandora’s Jar
stories.hook: Pandora had one sealed jar, one warning, and a question she could not leave alone.
stories.texture: The danger was not a monster at the door. It was the small, human urge to know what had been forbidden.
facts: Pandora was given a sealed jar in Greek tradition.
She opened it despite the warning.
Hardships escaped into the world.
Hope remained inside the jar.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Persephone and the Seasons
stories.hook: The moment Persephone vanished, the world itself began to starve.
stories.texture: Demeter’s grief did not stay private. It reached the fields, the harvest, and every living thing.
facts: Hades took Persephone to the underworld.
Demeter stopped making the earth fertile while searching for her.
Persephone ate pomegranate seeds.
Her periodic return explains the seasons in Greek tradition.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Phaethon and the Sun Chariot
stories.hook: Phaethon wanted proof that the sun god was his father, so he asked for the one gift no mortal could control.
stories.texture: The wish looks glorious for a heartbeat. Then the sky becomes a place where one mistake can burn the world below.
facts: Phaethon asked to drive the chariot of the sun for one day.
He could not control the horses.
The chariot’s path scorched parts of the earth.
Zeus struck Phaethon down to stop the disaster.

sources.title: Ovid, Metamorphoses, Book II
sources.variantNote: Use Ovid’s version as the anchor.

stories.title: Prometheus and the Stolen Fire
stories.hook: Before humans had warmth, light, or the tools to shape their lives, Prometheus made a choice that enraged Zeus.
stories.texture: For humans, fire meant more than warmth; it meant the power to make, survive, and begin again.
facts: Prometheus gave fire to humankind.
Zeus punished him by chaining him to the Caucasus.
An eagle consumed his liver each day.
In a well-known version, Heracles eventually freed him.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: Quetzalcoatl and the Return of Life
stories.hook: To bring life back, Quetzalcoatl had to descend into the realm of the dead.
stories.texture: The journey asks for more than courage. It asks a god to carry the remains of an older world into a new one.
facts: Quetzalcoatl is associated with the feathered serpent.
In one tradition, he descended to Mictlan to recover bones from earlier generations.
Humanity was created from those bones and his blood.
The story links sacrifice, creation, and renewal.

sources.title: Florentine Codex
sources.variantNote: Name the tradition as Nahua or Mexica when the source warrants it.

sources.title: Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas

stories.title: Ra’s Night Journey
stories.hook: Every sunset was only the beginning of Ra’s most dangerous journey.
stories.texture: Dawn was never guaranteed. Each morning had to be won again in the darkness beneath the horizon.
facts: Ra crossed the sky in a solar boat.
At night he traveled through the underworld.
There he faced the serpent Apophis.
His victory allowed dawn to return.

sources.title: Pyramid Texts
sources.variantNote: Accounts change across long historical periods.

stories.title: Ragnarök: The Twilight of the Gods
stories.hook: Norse myth does not promise the gods a peaceful ending; it promises a final day of reckoning.
stories.texture: Even the strongest gods know what is coming. That certainty is what gives the final battle its weight.
facts: Ragnarök is a prophesied series of catastrophic events.
Fenrir and other enemies break free.
Many gods fight and fall in the final battle.
After destruction, the world is described as renewed.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: The Churning of the Ocean
stories.hook: Gods and rivals pulled on the same cosmic rope, hoping the ocean would give up immortality.
stories.texture: Both sides wanted the same prize, so cooperation was always going to be temporary and dangerous.
facts: Devas and asuras churned the cosmic ocean to seek amrita.
They used Mount Mandara as a churning rod and Vasuki as a rope.
Many beings and treasures emerged from the ocean.
The quest for immortality became a struggle between rival groups.

sources.title: Ramayana
sources.variantNote: Identify the epic or Purana because versions vary widely.

stories.title: Savitri and Satyavan
stories.hook: Savitri knew the day her husband would die, and she chose him anyway.
stories.texture: When death finally arrives, the story does not make her stronger with a weapon. It gives her patience, language, and a refusal to turn back.
facts: Savitri chose Satyavan despite a prophecy that he would die within a year.
When Satyavan died, Yama took his soul.
Savitri followed Yama and spoke with him.
Yama granted boons that ultimately allowed Satyavan to return to life.

sources.title: Mahabharata, Vana Parva
sources.variantNote: Keep Savitri’s dialogue with Yama central.

stories.title: Shiva Drinks the Poison
stories.hook: The search for immortality first released something that could destroy the world.
stories.texture: Before anyone could claim immortality, someone had to face the poison no one else could contain.
facts: During the churning of the ocean, a deadly poison appeared.
Its danger threatened gods and worlds.
Shiva drank it to contain the poison.
His blue throat gave him the name Neelkanth in this tradition.

sources.title: Ramayana
sources.variantNote: Identify the epic or Purana because versions vary widely.

stories.title: Sigurd and Fafnir
stories.hook: Sigurd did not face Fafnir with an army. He waited alone beneath the path of a dragon.
stories.texture: The victory is only the first surprise, because the blood of the creature changes what Sigurd can hear and whom he can trust.
facts: Sigurd was urged to kill the dragon Fafnir.
He dug a pit and struck Fafnir from below.
After tasting the dragon’s blood, Sigurd understood birds in the tale.
The birds warned him about danger from Regin.

sources.title: Völsunga saga
sources.variantNote: Separate this heroic legend from the Eddic gods.

stories.title: Susanoo and the Serpent Orochi
stories.hook: One family faced a creature with eight heads, eight tails, and no obvious way to win.
stories.texture: The family had already lost daughters to the serpent. This was their last chance to stop the pattern.
facts: Susanoo found a family terrified of Orochi.
The creature had eight heads and eight tails.
Susanoo deceived it with strong sake.
He found the sword Kusanagi inside one of its tails.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.variantNote: Mark regional and later folkloric variants.

stories.title: Theseus and the Minotaur
stories.hook: A monster waited at the center of a maze built to make escape impossible.
stories.texture: The Labyrinth was not just a prison for a monster; it was designed to make anyone inside feel lost.
facts: Athens sent young people to Crete as tribute.
Theseus entered the Labyrinth to face the Minotaur.
Ariadne gave him a thread to find the way back.
He returned from the maze after killing the creature.

sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.variantNote: Versions differ by author and period.

stories.title: The Theft of Thor’s Hammer
stories.hook: Thor woke to discover that the weapon no giant could face had vanished.
stories.texture: Without Mjölnir, the thunder god was not only embarrassed; the balance between gods and giants felt exposed.
facts: The giant Thrym stole Mjölnir.
He demanded Freyja as the price for its return.
Thor disguised himself as Freyja to recover it.
When the hammer returned to his hands, he defeated the giants.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Týr and the Binding of Fenrir
stories.hook: To bind a wolf the gods feared, someone had to put a hand where no one else would.
stories.texture: The gods needed proof that Fenrir would trust them, even while they were preparing to betray that trust.
facts: The gods feared Fenrir’s growing strength.
They asked the wolf to test a magical binding.
Týr placed his hand in Fenrir’s mouth as a pledge.
Fenrir bit off Týr’s hand when he could not escape.

sources.title: Poetic Edda
sources.variantNote: Keep Eddic variants separate from later retellings.

stories.title: Xolotl and the Axolotl
stories.hook: When the gods prepared to die so the sun could move, one of them ran.
stories.texture: Xolotl’s story is strange because it turns fear itself into a shape that survives in water, visible and impossible to ignore.
facts: In one Nahua tradition, the gods had to sacrifice themselves to set the new sun in motion.
Xolotl feared death and tried to avoid the sacrifice.
He changed forms while fleeing.
One version connects his final transformation with the axolotl.

sources.title: Nahua traditions
sources.variantNote: Label this as a Nahua/Mexica variant when sourced.

stories.title: Yu and the Great Flood
stories.hook: For years, Yu watched floodwater take the land, and he chose not to fight it the way everyone else had.
stories.texture: His solution is not one spectacular battle. It is the slower, harder idea that sometimes survival means giving a force somewhere to go.
facts: A great flood threatened the land in Chinese tradition.
Yu worked to channel the waters rather than simply block them.
He traveled and labored for years in the story.
His success is linked with the founding of the Xia dynasty in later tradition.

sources.title: Shujing traditions and later accounts
sources.variantNote: Versions differ; identify the tradition used in final review.
